truth.papers.A18.title: Effect of alloying content on microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels
truth.papers.A18.paper_scope: Investigate the effects of C, Mn, and Al alloying content on the microstructure, density, and mechanical properties (tensile, impact toughness) of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels through systematic experimental characterization.

prediction.papers.A18.title: Effect of alloying content on microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels
prediction.papers.A18.paper_scope: Systematic experimental investigation of the effects of C, Mn, and Al contents on the microstructure, density, tensile properties, and Charpy impact toughness of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels after solution treatment.

truth.alloys.alloy_fe30mn11al1c.alloys_notes: Density 6.60 g/cm³; also used as reference base across three series.

truth.processes.proc_1050.description: Ingot homogenized at 1200°C for 5 h, hot rolled to 16 mm diameter rod, air cooled, then solution treated at 1050°C for 2 h followed by water quenching.
truth.processes.proc_1050.processes_notes: Common pre-treatment: vacuum induction melting (50 kg), casting into 150 mm ingot, homogenization 1200°C/5h, hot rolling to 16 mm rod, air cooling.
truth.processes.proc_1100.description: Ingot homogenized at 1200°C for 5 h, hot rolled to 16 mm diameter rod, air cooled, then solution treated at 1100°C for 2 h followed by water quenching.
truth.processes.proc_1100.processes_notes: Same prior steps as proc_1050. Applied only to Al-containing series for comparison.

prediction.processes.proc_1050.description: Vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1200°C for 5 h, hot rolling to 16 mm diameter rods, air cooling, solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h, water quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_1050.processes_notes: Standard solution treatment temperature used for the majority of specimens.
prediction.processes.proc_1100.description: Vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1200°C for 5 h, hot rolling to 16 mm diameter rods, air cooling, solution treatment at 1100°C for 2 h, water quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_1100.processes_notes: Elevated solution treatment temperature applied to selected Fe30Mn(x)Al1C specimens for comparison.

truth.structures.sample_c06.overall_structure: Dual-phase (austenite + ferrite) with nano-sized κ-carbides in austenite. Ferrite volume fraction ~30%, austenite grain refined by ferrite. Twin boundary fraction ~56.7%.
truth.structures.sample_c06.microstructure_lis: equiaxed grains, contains annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 56.7%)
truth.structures.sample_c10.overall_structure: Fully austenitic, no ferrite. κ-carbides with average size 3 nm. Twin boundary fraction 53.9%.
truth.structures.sample_c10.microstructure_lis: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 53.9%)
truth.structures.sample_c12.overall_structure: Fully austenitic. κ-carbides coarsened to ~20 nm. Twin boundary fraction 51.8%.
truth.structures.sample_c12.microstructure_lis: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 51.8%)
uniformly distributed in austenite matrix, rectangular shape observed
truth.structures.sample_c15.overall_structure: Fully austenitic. κ-carbides large (~50 nm). Twin boundary fraction 50.2%.
truth.structures.sample_c15.microstructure_lis: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 50.2%)
uniformly distributed in austenite matrix, rectangular morphology
truth.structures.sample_mn20.overall_structure: Mainly austenite with small amount of ferrite (3.3%). κ-carbides ~18 nm. Twin boundary fraction 47.8%.
truth.structures.sample_mn20.microstructure_li: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 47.8%)
truth.structures.sample_mn25.overall_structure: Austenite with trace ferrite (0.65%). κ-carbides ~5 nm. Twin boundary fraction 52.6%.
truth.structures.sample_mn25.microstructure_li: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 52.6%)
truth.structures.sample_mn30.overall_structure: Fully austenitic. κ-carbides ~3 nm. Twin boundary fraction 53.9%. (Same alloy as C-1.0)
truth.structures.sample_mn30.microstructure_li: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 53.9%)
truth.structures.sample_mn35.overall_structure: Fully austenitic. κ-carbides ~2 nm. Twin boundary fraction 59.6%.
truth.structures.sample_mn35.microstructure_li: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 59.6%)
truth.structures.sample_al6.overall_structure: Fully austenitic, no κ-carbides detected (or extremely small short-range ordering). Twin boundary fraction 45.3%.
truth.structures.sample_al6.microstructure_lis: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 45.3%)
truth.structures.sample_al8.overall_structure: Fully austenitic, no κ-carbides detected. Twin boundary fraction 50.3%.
truth.structures.sample_al8.microstructure_lis: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 50.3%)
truth.structures.sample_al10.overall_structure: Fully austenitic. κ-carbides ~1 nm. Twin boundary fraction 53.2%.
truth.structures.sample_al10.microstructure_li: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 53.2%)
truth.structures.sample_al11.overall_structure: Fully austenitic. κ-carbides ~3 nm (same alloy as C-1.0, Mn-30). Twin boundary fraction 53.9%.
truth.structures.sample_al11.microstructure_li: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 53.9%)
truth.structures.sample_al12.overall_structure: Austenite + small ferrite (3.08%). κ-carbides ~15 nm. Twin boundary fraction 51.3%.
truth.structures.sample_al12.microstructure_li: equiaxed grains, annealing twins (twin boundary fraction 51.3%)

truth.interfaces.idx_0.defect_interaction[0].q: κ-carbides are sheared during deformation, leading to weakened work hardening when sizes are large.
truth.interfaces.idx_0.phase_evolution: κ-carbide size increases with C and Al content, decreases with Mn content. Coarsening controlled by alloy content and solution treatment temperature.

prediction.interfaces.sample_c10.defect_interaction: Shear deformation facilitates glide of dislocations across κ-carbide shear planes, weakening work hardening.
prediction.interfaces.sample_c10.phase_evolution: κ-carbides precipitate from austenite via spinodal decomposition and ordering of C and Al; coarsen with increasing C and Al content.

truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.technique: Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) in SEM (FEI Quanta 650, 20 kV, step 0.5 μm)
truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.purpose: Phase distribution, grain size (linear intercept, >15°), twin boundary fraction analysis on all solution-treated samples.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.key_findi: Ferrite volume fraction varies; austenite grain size refined by ferrite; twin boundary fraction 45-60% depending on composition.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.technique: Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, 200 kV), dark field imaging, SAED
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.purpose: Characterization of κ-carbide size, distribution, morphology, and orientation relationship with austenite.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.key_findi: κ-carbides are coherent with austenite (001)κ||(001)γ; size increases with C and Al, decreases with Mn; morphology evolves to rectangular with coarsening.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.technique: Uniaxial tensile testing (MTS machine, initial strain rate 1.1×10⁻³ s⁻¹, ambient temperature)
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.purpose: Measure engineering stress-strain curves, yield strength, UTS, uniform and total elongation, work hardening rate.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.key_findi: Yield strength 401-1125 MPa; UTS 826-1191 MPa; total elongation 44-75%; work hardening rate max 1900-2300 MPa.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.technique: Charpy impact testing (MTS 500 J tester, -40°C, V-notch perpendicular to rod axis)
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.key_findi: Impact energy 1.5-354 J; strongly dependent on κ-carbide size; below 10 J for C>1.2%, Mn<25%, Al>10%.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_4.key_findi: Density range 6.52-7.12 g/cm³; linear dependence on C, Mn, Al mass%: ρ = 8.10–0.102C–0.10Al–0.01Mn.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_c10.key_: Measured densities ranged from 6.52 to 7.12 g/cm3 across the investigated alloy compositions.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Linear fitting of yield strength vs square root of κ-carbide size yields slope of 97.1 MPa/nm^0.5, consistent with particle shear strengthening mechanism.
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Particle shear model for κ-carbide strengthening
Yield strength contribution from κ-carbides in austenite matrix
α (particle nature constant), f (volume fraction of κ-carbides), d_Car (average κ-carbide diameter)

σ_shear = α f^(1/2) d_Car^(1/2). Active for carbide sizes up to at least 50 nm, significantly larger than typical ~5 nm transition to Orowan mechanism. Estimated flow stress without κ-carbide contribution ~478 MPa.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Density formula derived: ρ = 8.10 – 0.102C – 0.10Al – 0.01Mn (g/cm³, with alloy content in mass%). The coefficient for C (0.102) is much smaller than previously reported 0.41 g/cm³ per 1%C.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: True ultimate tensile stress σ_TS (average ~1350 MPa) and true fracture stress σ_FS (average ~2150 MPa) are nearly constant across all compositions, independent of microstructure or κ-carbide size. This behavior remains unexplained.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Yield strength (YS) shows a linear relationship with square root of κ-carbide diameter: YS (MPa) = 478 + 97.1 × d_Car^0.5 (nm^0.5). The slope 97.1 MPa/nm^0.5 indicates strong shear hardening. The intercept 478 MPa is attributed to solid solution and grain size strengthening.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Work hardening rate (WHR) decreases with increasing C and Al content, increases with Mn. Maximum WHR observed: 0.6C steel ~1900 MPa, 35Mn steel ~2300 MPa, 6Al steel ~2100 MPa. Except for 0.6C and 30Mn steels, WHR curves show a rapid decline followed by a slow rise and another decline.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Impact toughness at -40°C drops sharply when κ-carbide size exceeds ~4 nm (d_Car^0.5 >2). Only alloys with d_Car < ~4 nm (0.6C, 1.0C, Mn-35, Al-6, Al-8, Al-10) exhibit high impact toughness (>200 J).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Solution treatment at 1100°C (instead of 1050°C) on Al-series steels results in a slight decrease in yield strength, attributed to grain coarsening. The corresponding engineering stress-strain curves are shown in Fig. 2(d), but quantitative yield strengths are not reported. Approximate UTS from curves: Al-6~850 MPa, Al-8~850 MPa, Al-10~850 MPa, Al-11~850 MPa, Al-12~850 MPa; elongations >80% for 6-10Al, ~60% for 11-12Al.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: κ-carbide precipitation mechanism: spinodal decomposition and ordering of C and Al in austenite. κ-carbides exhibit a coherent interface with austenite and are sheared by dislocations during deformation (shear mechanism), consistent with particle shear model σ_shear = α f^(1/2) d_Car^(1/2).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: The presence of even small amounts of ferrite (e.g., 3.3% in Mn-20, 0.65% in Mn-25) effectively refines austenite grain size (9.9 μm vs. >22 μm in single-phase alloys), suggesting ferrite-induced grain boundary pinning.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Fracture behavior: uniform elongation and area reduction both linearly decrease with √d_Car, indicating that κ-carbide coarsening promotes early necking and reduces post-necking ductility.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Experimental details: All ingots were vacuum induction melted (50 kg), cast into 150 mm diameter, homogenized 1200°C/5h, hot rolled to 16 mm rod, air cooled. Tension tested at ambient temperature, strain rate 1.1×10⁻³ s⁻¹. Charpy V-notch specimens tested at -40°C.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: Table 2 footnotes: σ_TS is the maximum true stress just before necking (crack nucleation); σ_FS is the fracture true stress (local strength at break). Both are calculated from engineering values: σ_TS = σ_UTS(1+UE); σ_FS = σ_EF/(1-A).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_11.value: Grain size measurements used EBSD with boundary misorientation >15° (linear intercept). Twin boundary fraction f_Tw defined as total twin boundary length divided by total high-angle boundary length.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The dependence of alloy density on C, Mn, and Al content is formulated as ρ = 8.1 – 0.102C – 0.10Al – 0.01Mn g/cm3 (with alloy content in mass%), differing significantly from previously reported coefficients especially for C.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Both ultimate true stress (σ_TS) and fracture true stress (σ_FS) are nearly constant across all investigated compositions, with mean values around 1350 MPa and 2150 MPa respectively, regardless of κ-carbide size or ferrite fraction.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Work hardening rate behavior varies systematically: maximum work hardening rate around 1900 MPa for 0.6%C steel, 2300 MPa for 35%Mn steel, and 2100 MPa for 6%Al steel after 1050°C solution treatment.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Impact toughness at -40°C is strongly dependent on κ-carbide size; steels with high toughness (>200 J) are Fe30Mn6Al1C (354 J), Fe30Mn8Al1C (294 J), and Fe30Mn10Al1C (237 J).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: For Fe30Mn(x)Al1C specimens solution-treated at 1100°C for 2 h, only engineering stress-strain curves are presented in Fig. 2(d) with a slight decrease in yield strength relative to 1050°C, but no tabulated mechanical property data are provided.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: The κ-carbides exhibit a regular rectangular shape in the 1.5%C steel, with confined growth on {011} planes dictated by the austenite matrix crystal orientation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: In steels with 0.6%C and 30%Mn, the work hardening rate decreases monotonically with true strain, whereas other compositions show a rapid decline followed by a slow rise and then another decline.
